original_abstract: Daniel 1 chronicles the displacement of Daniel and his companions from Jerusalem to the Babylonian royal court, where they are groomed for imperial service. This narrative is fundamentally structured by geographic and symbolic references to space, which serve as a vehicle for the story’s underlying ideological conflict. The young men’s unwavering loyalty to Jerusalem—and by extension, the Temple and its cultic traditions—is directly challenged by the demands of the Babylonian court. This pressure is most clearly seen in the imposition of new names associated with foreign deities, representing an attempt at total cultural and religious assimilation. To resist this absorption and maintain their devotion to YHWH, the protagonists negotiate a dietary alternative to the king’s rations. By opting for specific food over the royal delicacies, they create a distinct "personal space" that preserves the spiritual boundaries between Jerusalem and Shinar. Ultimately, the spatial dynamics in Daniel 1 provide a framework for ancient Jewish readers to navigate the tension between maintaining a unique identity and surviving within a dominant, alien empire.
humanized_abstract: This unit follows Daniel and his friends being carried off to the court of the king of Babylon and being trained as some of his attendants. Space frames the action and is central to the ideological struggle of the chapter. The conflict is over where Daniel and his friends would worship. They worshipped Jerusalem and its Temple. The king of Babylon's court could be seen as being in far-off Shinar, the location of the Tower of Babel in Genesis 11, the most distant point from Jerusalem in the Bible and the location of some of the most foreign and heathen societies known to ancient Israel. The issue of worship is centred around names which the king imposed on all of the young men at the palace, names of his various pagan idols. Daniel and his friends request only a test of their ability to abstain from the palace diet, requesting instead food and water which would not be an affront to their God. By accepting their requested food and abstaining from drinking from the king's wine cellar Daniel and his friends create for themselves a "personal space" between themselves and the king which preserves the exclusive worship of YHWH as God of Israel, even in the most foreign of places, and safeguards their identity as members of the covenant community of Jerusalem and its Temple being the centre of that community in the world of Daniel. Through understanding the structure of Daniel 1 and the role of space in that structure, ancient Israelites/Jews could understand the conflict of Daniel 1 and appreciate the solution worked out by Daniel and his friends, the solution which allowed them to live as exiles in the strange land of the Medes while remaining faithful to the God of Jerusalem and the Temple.
